description: Rich, thick Nigerian beef stew with deeply fried tomato base and well-seasoned tender beef. Slower and bolder than chicken stew — the beef fat enriches the base in a way nothing else does.

ingredients.name: beef (chuck, shank or stew cuts)
ingredients.note: Tougher cuts like chuck and shank have more connective tissue — they break down beautifully in the stew and give it body. Avoid lean cuts like sirloin, they dry out.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Base of the stew. Fresh preferred, canned works.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: For body, sweetness and colour. Essential.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Beef absorbs heat well — 2 is a good middle ground.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling the beef, 1 into the blend, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: You need enough oil to fry the base properly. Beef fat will also render into the pot during frying.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: Beef stew takes more paste than chicken — the beef flavour is stronger and needs a deeper tomato base to match it.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: For both the beef seasoning and the stew base.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Add during parboiling. Remove before serving. Particularly good with beef.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: 1 for beef marinade, 2 for the stew. Crush before adding.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: Particularly recommended for beef stew. Adds depth the beef carries beautifully.

ingredients.name: ginger
ingredients.note: Pairs well with beef. Helps tenderise slightly during marination.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: For the beef marinade. Black pepper works too.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Optional in beef stew but adds a layer of umami depth underneath the beef flavour.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the beef well before parboiling. Taste and season the stew base separately.

ingredients.name: beef stock
ingredients.note: The liquid from parboiling the beef. Richer and more gelatinous than chicken stock — use every drop.

steps.title: Season and Marinate the Beef
steps.instruction: Beef rewards patience at this stage more than any other protein — the denser fibres need time to absorb seasoning all the way through. If the beef has not already been portioned, cut it into large bite-sized chunks, wash under cold water, and pat completely dry with paper towels — moisture on the surface dilutes the seasoning and later prevents a proper sear. Season generously with curry powder, thyme, white pepper, minced garlic, grated ginger, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and salt. Use your hands to mix and press the seasoning into every surface of every piece. Rest for at least 20 minutes at room temperature before cooking, though an overnight rest in the fridge produces noticeably more flavourful, deeply seasoned beef.
micro_steps: Cut beef into large bite-sized chunks — roughly 4–5cm — if not already portioned
Wash the beef chunks under cold running water and drain
Pat each piece completely dry with paper towels — dry beef seasons and sears better
Place all the beef in a large bowl
Add 1 tsp curry powder, 1½ tsp thyme, 1 tsp white pepper, all the minced garlic, and the grated ginger
Crush 1 seasoning cube into powder and add
Add a generous pinch of salt — about ½ tsp
Use your hands to mix everything together, pressing and rubbing the seasoning into every surface
Ensure every piece is evenly and thoroughly coated — no dry unseasoned patches
Cover the bowl with cling wrap or a plate and rest for at least 20 minutes at room temperature
For best results, refrigerate overnight — the flavour difference is significant
prep_notes: Blend the tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet before you start cooking — having it ready saves time when the base goes on
Mince the garlic and grate the ginger before handling raw meat so your hands stay clean
Pat the beef completely dry before seasoning — surface moisture prevents the seasoning from adhering and ruins the sear later
If marinating overnight, take the beef out of the fridge 20 minutes before cooking to take the chill off
steps.sensory_cue: Every piece of beef should be evenly and thoroughly coated — no pale unseasoned patches visible. The smell should be aromatic and deeply savoury, with garlic and thyme clearly prominent. After a long marination, the surface of the beef will look slightly tacky as the seasoning absorbs in.
steps.failure_risk: Beef is significantly denser than chicken and needs a longer marination to absorb seasoning all the way through the flesh. Rushing this step or adding too little seasoning at this stage produces bland, flat-tasting beef inside an otherwise well-seasoned stew — the outside will taste great but the interior of each chunk will be underwhelming.

steps.title: Parboil the Beef
steps.instruction: Place all the seasoned beef into a pot in a single layer if possible, add 1 roughly chopped onion and all the bay leaves, then pour in just enough water to barely cover the meat — keeping the water level low produces a much richer, more concentrated stock. Cook on medium-high heat, covered, until the beef is about 75% tender, meaning it yields to a fork with light pressure but still has noticeable resistance in the centre. Tougher cuts like chuck and shank will need the full 50–60 minutes; check at 40 minutes and continue if needed. Remove all the beef with a slotted spoon and reserve every drop of the stock — it will be darker, more gelatinous, and far richer than chicken stock.
micro_steps: Place all the seasoned beef into a large pot
Add 1 roughly chopped onion and all 3 bay leaves
Pour in just enough water to barely cover the beef — about 2–3cm above the meat
Cover the pot and bring to a boil over high heat
Once boiling, reduce to medium-high and start timing
Cook covered for 40 minutes, then pierce the thickest piece of beef with a fork
If the fork meets significant resistance, continue cooking and test every 10 minutes
The beef is ready at 75% done: the fork penetrates with light pressure but the centre still has some resistance
Remove all beef pieces with a slotted spoon and set aside on a plate
Pour the stock through a sieve into a bowl and reserve — do not discard
Remove and discard the bay leaves from the stock
steps.sensory_cue: The stock should be a deep golden-brown colour and smell intensely, unmistakably beefy. The surface will have a visible layer of fat floating on top — leave it in, it carries flavour. The beef pieces should look significantly softer than raw with a grey-brown exterior, but should still hold their shape without falling apart when lifted.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked beef entering the stew will remain tough and chewy in the finished dish — the relatively short final simmer is not long enough to tenderise it further. Chuck and shank genuinely need the full 50–60 minutes. Do not rush the parboil to save time; it costs you the quality of the final dish.

steps.title: Fry the Beef Pieces
steps.instruction: Heat half the vegetable oil in your widest, heaviest pot over high heat until it shimmers and a small drop of water sizzles immediately on contact. Fry the parboiled beef in batches — never more than a single uncrowded layer at a time — for 3–4 minutes per side until each piece has a deep, dark-brown crust on the outside. Resist the urge to move the pieces while they are searing; let them sit undisturbed so the crust forms properly. As the beef fries, its own fat will render out into the oil, adding a layer of rich beef flavour that will carry through the entire stew. Remove each batch to a plate and do not clean the pot between batches — all those drippings stay.
micro_steps: Pour half the vegetable oil — about 60ml — into your largest, heaviest pot
Heat over high heat until the oil shimmers visibly
Add the first batch of beef in a single, uncrowded layer — leave space between pieces
Let the beef sit completely undisturbed for 3–4 minutes — do not move or stir
Lift one piece to check the underside — it should be deep golden-brown, not pale grey
Flip each piece and fry the other side for another 3–4 minutes
Remove the browned beef to a plate
Repeat with the remaining beef in batches — do not add more oil unless the pot looks completely dry
After the last batch, turn off the heat
Look at the pot — there should be dark, flavourful drippings and rendered fat coating the bottom
Do not clean the pot — the next step starts here
steps.sensory_cue: Each piece should have a deep, dark golden-brown crust — not pale, not grey, genuinely brown. The rendered fat in the pot should look slightly golden and smell intensely savoury and beefy. The kitchen at this point should smell extraordinary — a deeply rich, almost smoky meat aroma.
steps.failure_risk: Crowding the pot is the single most common frying mistake. Too many pieces at once drops the oil temperature dramatically and the beef steams instead of searing — you end up with grey, flavourless meat with no crust and no caramelisation. Work in small batches no matter how long it takes; the flavour payoff is substantial.

steps.title: Build and Fry the Stew Base
steps.instruction: Without cleaning the pot, keep all the beef drippings and rendered fat right where they are and add the remaining oil if the pot looks dry. Fry the sliced onions directly in the drippings over medium-high heat, scraping up the caramelised fond from the bottom as they cook, until the onions are deep golden and very fragrant. Add tomato paste and fry vigorously for 3–4 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens from bright orange to a deep brick colour. Pour in the blended tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet, increase to high heat, and fry the entire base — stirring every 90 seconds — for 25–30 minutes. Beef stew requires a drier, more concentrated base than chicken stew because the stock you will add is already heavily gelatinous and rich.
micro_steps: Check the pot — add the remaining oil only if it looks dry; the beef drippings may be enough
Turn heat to medium-high and add the sliced onion rings
Stir immediately, scraping any caramelised beef fond off the bottom and into the onions
Fry the onions for 5–6 minutes until deep golden and very sweet-smelling
Add 3 tablespoons of tomato paste and stir vigorously — it will try to stick
Fry the paste for 3–4 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens to a deep brick-red
Pour in all the blended tomato, tatashe, and scotch bonnet mixture
Increase heat to high and stir everything together
Fry the base at high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 25–30 minutes
At the 20-minute mark, drag a spoon through the base — it should be thick and almost paste-like
The base is fully ready when it looks very dark brick-red, appears almost dry at the pot edges, and oil has risen clearly to the surface
steps.sensory_cue: The finished base should be very thick, almost paste-like, and look dark brick-red. Oil should be clearly floating on the surface. The edges of the base near the pot wall should look almost dry. Any raw or sour tomato smell must be completely gone — if you can still smell raw tomato, it needs more time.
steps.failure_risk: Beef stew requires a drier, more concentrated base than chicken stew — the beef stock you will add is already very rich and gelatinous, and a watery or insufficiently fried base will make the finished stew greasy and slightly sour. Do not cut the frying time short. The oil visibly rising to the surface is the non-negotiable signal that the base is ready.

steps.title: Season the Base and Add Stock
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the remaining curry powder, thyme, and ground crayfish if using, stirring each one in thoroughly. Crush the remaining 2 seasoning cubes to powder and stir in. Taste the base before reaching for salt — the beef stock you are about to add is already rich and seasoned. Pour in the reserved beef stock, stir everything together well, and bring to a steady simmer. Taste the seasoned base with the stock incorporated and make any adjustments now, before the beef goes back in.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Add 1 tsp curry powder and stir into the base
Add 1½ tsp thyme and stir in
Add 1 tablespoon of ground crayfish if using and stir in
Crush 2 remaining seasoning cubes to powder and add — stir until fully dissolved
Taste the base at this point — assess its salt level before adding the stock
Pour in 1½ cups of the reserved beef stock
Stir everything together thoroughly
Bring to a steady simmer over medium heat
Taste the combined base and stock — it should be rich, bold, and deeply savoury
Add salt only if genuinely needed — the beef stock may have already provided enough
Adjust seasoning now before the beef goes in — this is the last easy moment to taste accurately
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base with beef stock should look noticeably thicker than the equivalent stage in chicken stew — the gelatine from the beef bones and connective tissue makes it almost glossy. The colour should be a deep, rich dark orange-red. The aroma should be bold, beefy, and deeply savoury.
steps.failure_risk: Beef stock is considerably richer and more concentrated than chicken stock, and often carries more residual salt from the marinade. Tasting carefully before adding salt is critical here — the finished stew is very easy to over-salt at this stage, and there is no simple fix once it happens.

steps.title: Add Beef and Finish
steps.instruction: Add all the fried beef pieces back into the stew, stirring gently to ensure every piece is coated in the rich base. Cover the pot and simmer on low heat for 20–25 minutes, lifting the lid to stir gently every 5 minutes. The beef will finish cooking through, absorb the deep flavours of the stew base, and become completely tender. The stew will thicken further around the beef as it simmers. If at any point the stew looks too thick or is catching on the bottom, add a small splash of water — not more stock. Do a final salt check, remove any remaining bay leaves, and serve.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to low before adding the beef
Add all the fried beef pieces to the pot
Stir gently with a folding motion to coat every piece in the stew base
Cover the pot with a lid
Simmer on low heat — set a timer for 5 minutes
Every 5 minutes, lift the lid, stir gently from the bottom, and replace the lid
After 15 minutes, check the beef — pierce the thickest piece with a fork
It should yield completely with no resistance and cut easily
Check the consistency — the stew should be thick and coating the beef heavily
If it looks too thick or is catching, add a small splash of water and stir
Do a final salt taste — adjust if needed
Turn off heat and rest for 5 minutes before serving — the stew thickens slightly as it sits
steps.sensory_cue: Beef pieces should be completely tender — soft enough to cut easily with the side of a spoon. The stew should be thick, glossy, and deeply coloured, coating every piece of beef heavily. A layer of oil sitting on the surface is the sign of a properly made Nigerian stew — do not skim it off.
steps.failure_risk: High heat during the final simmer dries out the beef and can cause the stew to split — the oil separates aggressively and the sauce becomes grainy. Keep the heat genuinely low from this point. If the stew thickens too much, add a splash of plain water rather than more stock, which would make it too salty.
